title: Birth of Swiss folk poet Jakob Stutz
description: Jakob Stutz, later known as a folk poet and school reformer, is born in Switzerland. Throughout his life he wrestled privately with his attraction to men in an era that criminalized it.

title: Heinrich Hössli self-publishes Eros, an early defense of male same-sex love
description: Heinrich Hössli brings out the two volumes of "Eros, die Männerliebe der Griechen" at his own expense, volume one in Glarus, volume two in St. Gallen. The work is among the earliest modern European texts to argue for the acceptance of love between men.

title: Karl Heinrich Ulrichs calls for repeal of anti-homosexual laws in Munich
description: Karl Heinrich Ulrichs, widely regarded as the first man to live openly as a homosexual, addresses the Congress of German Jurists in Munich, demanding the abolition of all criminal penalties for homosexual acts. His speech is considered the first public plea of its kind.

title: Karl Maria Kertbeny coins the term "homosexual"
description: Hungarian writer Karl Maria Kertbeny uses the word "homosexual" for the first time, in a pamphlet arguing against the criminalization of same-sex acts under Prussian law. The term later becomes the standard word in medicine, law, and everyday speech.

title: First federal commission on a unified Swiss penal code convenes
description: The Swiss federal councils appoint a first commission to draft a single national penal code. Jacob Rudolf Forster, together with other homosexual men, lobbies the process for impunity for same-sex acts. The codification effort stretches nearly fifty years, until the code takes effect in 1942.

title: Birth of Karl Meier, later founder of Der Kreis
description: Karl Meier is born. Under the pseudonym "Rolf" he later founds and leads the organization KREIS in Zurich along with its trilingual periodical Der Kreis - Le Cercle - The Circle, one of the most influential homophile publications of the mid-20th century.

title: Magnus Hirschfeld founds the Scientific-Humanitarian Committee in Berlin
description: Dr. Magnus Hirschfeld establishes the Wissenschaftlich-humanitäres Komitee in Berlin, the world's first organization campaigning for homosexual rights. Its program drew on the earlier writings of Karl Heinrich Ulrichs and Heinrich Hössli.

title: Adolf Brand launches Der Eigene, the world's first gay periodical
description: Adolf Brand, a member of the Scientific-Humanitarian Committee, begins self-publishing Der Eigene in Berlin, the first magazine in the world aimed at homosexual readers. It appears with some regularity until 1932.

title: Jacob Rudolf Forster self-publishes a protest against sodomy convictions
description: Jacob Rudolf Forster of St. Gallen publishes his book "Justizmorde im 19. Jahrhundert" in Zurich at his own expense, an outcry against the judicial treatment of homosexual men in 19th-century Switzerland.

title: Swiss Federal Council tasks experts with revising the penal code draft
description: The Federal Council assigns an expert commission to continue work on the new national penal code, building on the proposals of the first commission. The draft's treatment of same-sex acts remains one of the open questions.

title: Preliminary Swiss penal code draft sparks debate on homosexuality
description: The preliminary draft of the new Swiss penal code is presented. Its paragraphs on homosexuality trigger a public discussion about the causes of same-sex attraction.

title: Third expert commission drafts a new Swiss penal code
description: A third expert commission takes up the work of producing a fresh draft for Switzerland's unified national penal code, continuing the decades-long codification process that would eventually decriminalize same-sex acts.

title: Federal Council submits draft for a national Swiss penal code
description: The Swiss Federal Council presents its draft for a unified national penal code (StGB) to parliament. The move opens the political battle over its contested provisions, among them the question of whether homosexual acts should be criminalized nationwide.

title: Canton of Basel-Stadt decriminalizes homosexuality
description: The revised penal law of the canton of Basel-Stadt comes into force, ending the prosecution of homosexual acts in Basel more than two decades before decriminalization applied across Switzerland.

title: First Swiss homosexual organization founded in Lucerne
description: A group of men in Lucerne establishes Switzerland's first homosexual association, renamed within the year to "Schweizer Freundschaftsbund / Société amicale suisse". Its two-day founding meeting marks the start of organized gay life in the country.

title: Schweizer Freundschaftsbund opens its own clubhouse in Zurich
description: The Schweizer Freundschaftsbund moves into premises of its own in Zurich's old town, giving Switzerland's early homosexual movement its first dedicated meeting place.

title: Schweizer Freundschaftsbund renamed Schweizer Bund für Menschenrechte
description: At its general assembly in Zurich, the Schweizer Freundschaftsbund adopts the new name "Schweizer Bund für Menschenrechte" (Swiss League for Human Rights), reframing the homosexual cause in the language of human rights.

title: Schweizer Bund für Menschenrechte fades out of existence
description: The Schweizer Bund für Menschenrechte disappears from published records, marking the apparent end of Switzerland's first homosexual organization after roughly four years of activity.

title: Swiss National Council debates penal code draft decriminalizing homosexuality
description: The National Council, the lower chamber of the Swiss parliament, deliberates the draft of the new national penal code, which foresees the removal of criminal penalties for homosexual acts.

title: Women's club Amicitia founded in Zurich
description: The Damen-Club "Amicitia" is established in Zurich, creating an early organized meeting place for lesbian women in Switzerland.

title: Swiss Council of States approves the new penal code
description: Following its deliberations, the Council of States, the upper chamber of the Swiss parliament, votes in favor of the new national penal code, keeping the decriminalization of homosexual acts on track.

title: Excentric-Club Zürich founded for men
description: The "Excentric-Club Zürich" (E.C.Z.), a social club for homosexual men, is established in Zurich, adding to the city's growing club scene of the early 1930s.

title: Book and literature club founded in Basel on New Year's Eve
description: On New Year's Eve, the "Bücherfreunde & Literaturclub Basel" is founded, a literary society that served as a discreet gathering point for homosexual men in Basel.

title: Freundschafts-Banner, first Swiss homosexual magazine, appears
description: The first issue of Freundschafts-Banner is published, making it Switzerland's first periodical for homosexual readers and the starting point of a publishing lineage that later produced Der Kreis.

title: Actor Karl Meier returns from Berlin to Switzerland
description: The actor Karl Meier, later editor of Der Kreis under the name "Rolf", moves back to Switzerland from Berlin, where the political climate for homosexual men was rapidly deteriorating.

title: Short-lived men's club Freundschaftsbund Zürich formed
description: The men's club "Freundschaftsbund Zürich" (FBZ) is formed in Zurich but folds again shortly after its creation.

title: Mixed association Amicitia founded in Zurich for women and men
description: The "Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Verband Amicitia" (S.Fr.V.) is founded in Zurich, open to both women and men — a rarity among the homosexual associations of the period.

title: Freundschafts-Banner relaunched as Schweizerisches Freundschafts-Banner
description: After the original run of Freundschafts-Banner ended within months in 1932, the magazine returns in Zurich under the new title Schweizerisches Freundschafts-Banner, continuing Switzerland's first homosexual periodical.

title: New club formed under the name Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Verband
description: Alongside the magazine relaunch, a new club calling itself Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Verband (SFB) is created. The source record is thin on how this body related to the Amicitia association founded the same month.

title: Short-lived branches open in Basel and Lucerne
description: Local sections of the Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Bund are set up in Basel and Lucerne, but both dissolve again after a short time.

title: Karl Meier applies for club membership as Rudolf Rheiner
description: Using the pseudonym Rudolf Rheiner, Karl Meier applies to join the Schweizerischer Freundschafts-Verband — the beginning of his involvement with the organization he would later lead as "Rolf".

title: Organization renamed Schweizerische Liga für Menschenrechte
description: The Schweizerischer Freundschaftsbund takes the new name "Schweizerische Liga für Menschenrechte" (Swiss League for Human Rights), once again presenting the homosexual cause under a human-rights banner.

title: Magazine Freundschafts-Banner renamed Menschenrecht
description: The Swiss homosexual periodical Freundschafts-Banner changes its title to Menschenrecht ("Human Right"), matching its publisher's human-rights framing.

title: Swiss parliament adopts the national penal code
description: Both chambers of the Swiss federal parliament pass the national penal code (StGB), whose provisions end the criminalization of consensual homosexual acts between adults once the code takes effect.

title: Swiss voters approve the penal code in a national referendum
description: After opponents forced a referendum, the Swiss electorate votes to accept the new federal penal code, confirming the decriminalization of homosexual acts contained in it.

title: Schweizerische Liga für Menschenrechte holds its final assembly
description: The Schweizerische Liga für Menschenrechte convenes its last general assembly; the organization is dissolved shortly afterwards.

title: Mass trial of 74 homosexual men in St. Gallen
description: A court in St. Gallen tries 74 homosexual men in a single proceeding, handing down severe sentences — one of the largest prosecutions of homosexual men in Swiss history, on the eve of national decriminalization.

title: Women's pages in Menschenrecht appear as Die Freundin, then cease
description: The women's section of the magazine Menschenrecht is given its own title, Die Freundin, but is discontinued at the end of the year for lack of interest.

title: Switzerland decriminalizes homosexual acts as the penal code takes effect
description: The new federal penal code comes into force, ending the criminal prosecution of consensual homosexual acts between adults across Switzerland — the culmination of a codification process begun in the 1890s, though the age of consent remained unequal.

title: Karl Meier takes over Menschenrecht; publisher becomes DER KREIS
description: Karl Meier succeeds Anna Vock as editor of the magazine Menschenrecht, and its publishing house adopts the name DER KREIS — the beginning of the magazine's transformation into the internationally read Der Kreis.

title: Menschenrecht adds a regular French-language section
description: From April onward, the Swiss homosexual magazine Menschenrecht carries a regular French-language section, extending its reach into francophone Switzerland.

title: Subscribers form the first Tischrunden DER KREIS gatherings
description: Readers of Menschenrecht begin organizing regular social round tables called "Tischrunden DER KREIS", first in Zurich, then in Bern, Biel, and Chur — the social network that underpinned the Der Kreis community.

title: Der Kreis - Le Cercle launches as a bilingual monthly
description: The magazine Menschenrecht is succeeded by the new bilingual monthly Der Kreis - Le Cercle, distributed through a newly created subscriber organization, the Lesezirkel DER KREIS - LE CERCLE. The publication became the leading homophile magazine of its era in Europe.

title: DER KREIS meeting point opens in Basel
description: A local DER KREIS meeting point is established in Basel, operating with interruptions until the end of 1946.

title: Short-lived DER KREIS meeting point in Davos
description: A DER KREIS meeting point opens in Davos but survives for only a few months.

title: LE CERCLE Lausanne founded, lasts only months
description: LE CERCLE Lausanne, styled "Rendez-vous des camarades", is founded as a French-speaking offshoot of the Der Kreis network, but dissolves after only a few months.

title: DER KREIS meeting point opens in St. Gallen
description: A DER KREIS meeting point is set up in St. Gallen, remaining active until 1948.

title: DER KREIS Zurich moves into the Eintracht at Theater am Neumarkt
description: DER KREIS in Zurich obtains new club rooms, the "Eintracht" at the Theater am Neumarkt, which became the home of the organization's legendary gatherings.

title: WHO lists homosexuality as a disease in its international classification
description: The World Health Organization places homosexuality on its International Classification of Diseases, giving the pathologization of same-sex attraction official global sanction — a listing not fully removed until 1990.

title: First International Congress for Sexual Equality meets in Amsterdam
description: Delegates gather in Amsterdam for the first International Congress for Sexual Equality (ICSE), an early attempt to build a cross-border homophile movement in postwar Europe.
